coredata.dc:title: An agar hydrogel-CuNPs/N@CQDs dual-mode colorimetric/fluorescent indicator for non-destructive monitoring of banana ripening

coredata.dc:creator: Lohrasbi Nejad, Sepideh
Shekarchizadeh, Hajar
coredata.dc:description: A colorimetric-fluorescence ripeness indicator, based on copper nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen (CuNPs/N@CQDS) immobilized in agar hydrogel (AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs) for ethylene gas detection, was developed for detecting the ripening of bananas. Ethylene could reduce the fluorescence intensity of CuNPs/N@CQDs and cause a red shift of the LSPR peak. The prepared AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator had an irreversible response to ethylene with LOD and LOQ of 9.94 and 30.12 μM, respectively. The results of XPS and FTIR confirmed the formation of Cu-OH bonds. The color of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator changed from pale green to yellow and ultimately to brown during banana ripening. There was a strong correlation between the decrease in firmness of banana tissue, the increase in reducing sugars, and the change in the indicator's color. Therefore, the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator is capable of simple, low-cost, high-accuracy, and naked-eye identification of the fruit's ripening in intelligent packaging.

head.ce:title: An agar hydrogel-CuNPs/N@CQDs dual-mode colorimetric/fluorescent indicator for non-destructive monitoring of banana ripening

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, College of Agriculture, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, College of Agriculture, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A colorimetric-fluorescence ripeness indicator, based on copper nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen (CuNPs/N@CQDS) immobilized in agar hydrogel (AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs) for ethylene gas detection, was developed for detecting the ripening of bananas. Ethylene could reduce the fluorescence intensity of CuNPs/N@CQDs and cause a red shift of the LSPR peak. The prepared AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator had an irreversible response to ethylene with LOD and LOQ of 9.94 and 30.12 μM, respectively. The results of XPS and FTIR confirmed the formation of Cu-OH bonds. The color of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator changed from pale green to yellow and ultimately to brown during banana ripening. There was a strong correlation between the decrease in firmness of banana tissue, the increase in reducing sugars, and the change in the indicator's color. Therefore, the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator is capable of simple, low-cost, high-accuracy, and naked-eye identification of the fruit's ripening in intelligent packaging.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Copper nanoparticles and carbon quantum dot doped with nitrogen nanocomplex

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging is an essential step in the chain of food supply as it plays an important role in preventing contamination and maintaining food quality. Considering the limitations of conventional food packaging, which can only protect food products from the external environment without having the ability to supply extra information about food, intelligent food packaging systems are of great interest because they can monitor the quality of the product and its storage conditions from factories to delivery to the end consumer in real-time. These systems rely on sensors and indicators, which are vital tools to ensure food safety (Alam et al., 2021; Dikmetas et al., 2023; Khan et al., 2023; Miao et al., 2023; Zhai et al., 2019).

Colorimetric sensors and indicators are visible tools in food packages whose color changes after reacting with analytes, directly reflecting the freshness of the packaged food (Dikmetas et al., 2023; Shao et al., 2021). The application of nanotechnology in food packaging systems has resulted in the improvement of colorimetric indicators using metal nanoparticles (Ahari & Soufiani, 2021; Alberti et al., 2021). Plasmonic metal nanoparticles (gold, silver, and copper) have garnered significant interest owing to their unique physical and chemical properties, particularly the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) property. This property can be used to develop analytical methods, such as optical measurement (Hatamie et al., 2014; He et al., 2022). Copper nanoparticles have the potential to be considered more favorable than gold and silver nanoparticles because they are inexpensive and have low optical losses in the visible and NIR regions (Kailasa et al., 2018; Katyal, 2021).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a recently developed category of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. They have garnered significant interest because of their distinctive properties, including affordability, chemical stability, unique optical properties, non-toxicity, and excellent compatibility (Gan et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2017; Praoboon et al., 2022). Doping carbon quantum dots with nitrogen atoms improves their quantum yield (Jourshabani et al., 2024; Sajwan et al., 2024). Additionally, integrating CQDs with metal nanoparticles enhances the color changes, electronic properties, and LSPR properties of the nanoparticles owing to the abundance of functional groups available to bind to analytes (Li et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019).

Due to the lack of regulatory infrastructure during the fruit processing stage, it is challenging to guarantee the quality and freshness of fruits and vegetables. As a result, the use of colorimetric indicators in fruit packaging can lead to positive outcomes, including improved detection of fruit quality, public health, and waste reduction (Dikmetas et al., 2023). Using this system, it is possible to identify aspects of fruit quality such as freshness, ripeness, spoilage, and microbial contamination (Santoso et al., 2023). Notably, as fruits mature and spoil, they release a variety of compounds, including ethylene gas (Luo et al., 2022). Ethylene (CH2 = CH2) is the smallest phytohormone and a natural growth-promoting gas that is colorless and odorless. It has an essential role in several growth steps, including respiration, seed germination, fruit ripening, senescence, plant and fruit stress responses, and abscission, especially in climacteric fruits like bananas, apples, tomatoes, and sweet melons (Esser & Swager, 2010; Romero & Lafuente, 2022; Tabassum et al., 2021; Xiao et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2018).

Banana fruit, ranked as the fourth most widely consumed globally, is produced in tropical and subtropical regions. It is rich in carbohydrate compounds, ascorbic acid, β-carotene, soluble sugars, phenols, and minerals such as potassium and calcium. At the onset of banana fruit ripening, rapid respiration and catalytic ethylene production lead to the loss of quality characteristics like color, texture, and taste, thus reducing shelf life (Brat et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2014). Therefore, due to the limitations of the physicochemical reactions of ethylene, changes in fruit maturity must be monitored using high-performance indicators (Ko & Rogers, 2021; Zhao et al., 2020).

Several research has been conducted on the application of colorimetric indicators for the detection of ethylene gas. Santoso et al. (2023) used an indicator based on a complex of cassava starch-chitosan and anthocyanin from dragon fruit skin and Gambier catechin to monitor bananas ripening. They concluded that the banana fruit produces volatile compounds during ripening that change the internal pH of the package and, as a result, lead to a color change of the indicator from dark red to orange (Santoso et al., 2023). Other reports include the use of a palladium-bathophenanthroline complex in detecting ethylene gas in kiwifruit (Hu et al., 2016), the use of a molybdenum chromophore in detecting ripeness and the amount of ethylene gas in apples (Lang & Hübert, 2012), the use of Brazilian Sapan wood extract in banana fruit (Kurnianto et al., 2020), and the use of ammonium molybdate to detect ethylene gas (Warsiki et al., 2020). However, no study has been conducted using plasmonic noble metal nanoparticles for ethylene gas detection.

According to previous studies, the copper atom acts as an essential cofactor for binding ethylene gas in fruits and vegetables (Esser & Swager, 2010). In our previous work (Lohrasbi Nejad & Shekarchizadeh, 2024), we utilized plasmonic copper metal nanoparticles enhanced with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CuNPs/N@CQDs) to determine the shelf life of fish based on their response to hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) gas produced during spoilage. Given the presence of copper in the structure of this indicator, the idea was conceived to explore its potential for assessing the colorimetric and fluorescence detection of ethylene gas during banana ripening based on the LSPR mechanism. Accordingly, in this research, for the first time, a ripeness indicator based on agar hydrogel containing copper nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen (AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs) was designed to detect ethylene gas released from bananas stored at room and refrigerator temperatures. In our preliminary experiments, we tested other substrates (cellulose, gelatin, and guar) instead of agar. However, after stabilizing the CuNPs/N@CQDs within these materials, the prepared indicator showed no response to ethylene gas, probably due to their insufficient porosity or incompatibility with the nanomaterials. Agar hydrogel, by contrast, possesses an optimal porous structure that facilitates efficient gas diffusion while maintaining the stability of the embedded nanomaterials. Additionally, the inherent hydrophilic nature of agar hydrogel enhances the interaction between the gas molecules and the indicator, further improving the sensitivity of the system. Therefore, we showed that using this novel material offers significant advantages, including enhanced stability, improved sensitivity, and a clear, irreversible colorimetric and fluorescent response. Furthermore, the current indicator demonstrates enhanced functionality for tracking firmness and sugar content in bananas, establishing a robust correlation between biochemical changes in the fruit and the indicator's dual-mode response. By addressing the distinct challenges of fruit ripening detection, this study expands the scope of intelligent packaging and demonstrates substantial novelty compared to previous studies.

Bananas were purchased from a local market in Isfahan, Iran. Agar powder and other chemical components, including Cu (NO3)2, NaOH, hydrazine hydrate (N2H4), citric acid, polyethyleneimine (PEI), ethanol, hexane, acetic acid, potassium sodium tartrate, 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid, hydrochloric acid, glucose, sodium sulfide (Na2S), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), and disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4•12H2O), were obtained from Merck KGaA Co., Ltd. (Darmstadt, Germany). Ethephon was purchased from Zistazma Biotechnology Co. (Mashhad, Iran).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize CuNPs/N@CQDs, as described in our previous work (Lohrasbi Nejad & Shekarchizadeh, 2024). For the N-doped CQDs (N@CQDs) solution synthesis, PEI (0.945 g) as a surface-modifying agent containing nitrogen and citric acid (0.1 g) as a carbon source were mixed in deionized water (30 mL) for one hour. Next, 14 mL of a 0.1 M solution of Cu (NO3)2 as the source for CuNPs preparation, 5 mL of a 0.005 M solution of NaOH as a pH-adjusting and CuNPs-stabilizing agent, and 2 mL of N2H4 as the reducing agent were mixed with the N@CQDs solution, and the mixture was heated (180 °C/20 h) in a Teflon-lined autoclave to synthesize the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex by hydrothermal processing.

The CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex was characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) system (CM 120, Philips, Netherlands) and a zeta sizer (ZEN3600, Horiba, Germany). The changes in the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex after being exposed to banana ripening were also investigated.

The fluorescence behavior of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex was investigated by applying a fluorometric device (SHIMADZU, RF-5000, Japan) within the 200–700 nm excitation wavelengths at 50 nm intervals. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I) was determined using Eq. 1. (Wen et al., 2019).

Where I1 and I2 represent the fluorescence intensities of the CuNPs/N@CQDs prior to and after being exposed to banana ripening, respectively.

Also, changes in the fluorescence spectrum of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution over time, resulting from exposure to ethylene gas produced during banana ripening on storage days (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10), were investigated using a fluorometer.

The LSPR properties of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex were investigated by recording its absorption spectra prior to and after being exposed to banana ripening by applying a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Unico 2100, USA) within the 200–700 nm. The parameter of sensitivity (S) of CuNPs/N@CQDs to ethylene was determined using Eq. 2.
(2)
S = ∆ λ / ∆ h
Where Δλ is the maximum absorption wavelength difference, and Δh is the refractive index difference of CuNPs/N@CQDs prior to and after being exposed to banana ripening (Afsari & Sarraf, 2020).
Elemental analysis of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution was performed by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis (PerkinElmer, Optima 8300, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and analysis of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs colorimetric indicator
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous study, the colorimetric indicator was prepared (Lohrasbi Nejad & Shekarchizadeh, 2024). First, an agar solution (2 % w/v) was prepared and then mixed with the prepared CuNPs/N@CQDs solution at a 1:1 ratio on a heater stirrer for one hour (100 °C/500 rpm). The mixture was casted on a 6 cm diameter Petri dish and left to dry at room temperature for 24 h. In the final step, the prepared indicators, based on agar hydrogel and CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex (AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs), were cut into 2 cm diameter circles for subsequent experiments (Lohrasbi Nejad & Shekarchizadeh, 2024; Yu et al., 2022).

In order to determine and confirm the constituent elements of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator, elemental analysis and element distribution were performed using an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Octan Elite, AMETEK, UK).

To produce ethylene gas, an ethephon (C2H6ClO3P) solution was used according to the method of Zhang et al. (2010). Firstly, a 1 M solution of ethephon in ethanol was prepared. Different concentrations of the ethephon solution (0.05, 0.1, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4, 4.5 M) were mixed with disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer (5 mM) to decompose ethephon and produce ethylene according to Eq. 3.
(3)

Then, AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicators were positioned on top of a glass vial filled with different concentrations of ethylene gas. The color characteristics, brightness/darkness (L*), redness/greenness (a*), and yellowness/blueness (b*) of the indicators were recorded after 24 h by applying a colorimeter. (Nippon Denshoku, ZE6000, Japan). Eq. 4 was used to calculate the ΔE parameter (Zhang & Wen, 2010).

where L1*, a1*, and b1* represent the color characteristics of the original indicator, and L2*, a2*, and b2* correspond to the color characteristics of the indicator following exposure to different concentrations of ethylene.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were determined using Eqs. 5 and 6, respectively (Teymouri & Shekarchizadeh, 2022).
(5)
LOD = 3.3 σ / S
(6)
LOQ = 10 σ / S
where, σ
is the standard deviation (n = 10), and S is the slope of the fitted line of the calibration curve.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicators prior to and after being exposed to banana ripening were recorded using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Jasco, FTIR6300, Japan) and an XPS spectrometer (Specs, Slexps, Germany), respectively, to analyze the chemical changes of the indicator after the reaction with ethylene. Analysis of the results was conducted using Origin Pro software.

The precise reaction of an indicator to the analyte is an important criterion in evaluating its efficiency. Therefore, the response of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator to other chemicals produced during banana ripening was investigated. For this purpose, the color changes of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicators were recorded after being exposed to different volatile compounds, including ethanol, hydrogen sulfide, hexane, carbon dioxide, methane, and acetic acid. To investigate the effect of the simultaneous presence of other volatile compounds on the color of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator in response to ethylene, a constant concentration of 2.5 M ethephon was used along with 5, 10, 50, and 100 μM concentrations of ethanol, hydrogen sulfide, hexane, and acetic acid, and the color characteristics were measured.

In order to evaluate the response time of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator to ethylene gas, the indicator was exposed to the lowest detectable concentration of ethylene gas, and its colorimetric properties were evaluated at the same time interval. The response time was considered the time when the color changes (ΔE) became stable and were not significant.

The stability of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator at different temperatures (−18, 4, and 25 °C) for 21 days was evaluated by measuring the L*, a*, and b* parameters at intervals of every 7 days and was compared with the original indicator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator for the monitoring of banana ripening
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh bananas were placed in polypropylene packages, and an AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator was connected to the lid of the packages. The packages were kept at ambient and refrigerator temperatures (25 °C and 4 °C) for ten days. The color characteristics of the colorimetric indicator and the quality of the bananas were assessed daily throughout the storage.

In order to investigate the color changes of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator in case of a damaged package, when exposed to the banana fruit ripening process, a small slit with a diameter of 3 cm was made on the packages containing bananas, and the color changes of the indicators were examined during the shelf life at ambient and refrigerator temperatures.

In order to investigate the quality of stored bananas, the amount of reducing sugars in bananas was determined based on the 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid (DNS) method. Briefly, 2.5 g of banana tissue was mixed with distilled water (50 mL) at 80 °C for 2 h and then filtered. Next, 1 mL of the filtrate was boiled with 1 mL of DNS solution for 5 min. The color changed from yellow to red. After the solution reached ambient temperature, 8 mL of distilled water was added, and its absorbance was recorded against a control at λ = 540 nm. The reducing sugar content was reported using a glucose standard curve (100–400 ppm). To prepare the DNS solution, 1 g of sodium hydroxide was combined with 70 mL of distilled water. Subsequently, 30 g of potassium tartrate were added to the solution. After homogenization, 1 g of DNS was gradually added to the solution while stirring continuously. In the next step, 0.05 g of sodium sulfite was added, and the final volume was adjusted to 100 mL. The color of banana skin stored at ambient and refrigerator temperatures for 10 days was recorded using a colorimeter device at three random points on the banana skin. A penetrometer was used to measure the bananas' firmness by applying a universal testing machine (STM-20. Santam, Iran) using a probe with a diameter of 8 mm at a penetration depth of 10 mm. Banana firmness was expressed in terms of N (Huang et al., 2014).

An analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, followed by Fisher's least significant difference (LSD) test at a confidence level of 95 %. Data analysis was conducted using SAS version 9. Three replications for all experiments were conducted.

Fig. 1a shows the morphology, particle size, and size distribution of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex solution prior to and after being exposed to banana ripening. The primary CuNPs/N@CQDs nanoparticles were spherical, and their average size was 4.23 ± 1.15 nm. Fig. 1b indicates that nanoparticles are mostly 3–5 nm, suggesting the suitability of the hydrothermal method for nanoparticle synthesis. Qiao et al. (2015) stated that the spherical nanoparticles of CuNPs/N@CQDs were 4–7 nm. After exposing the nanoparticle solution to banana ripening, the distance between nanoparticles decreased due to the interaction of CuNPs/N@CQDs nanoparticles with ethylene, causing nanoparticle accumulation and increasing their size (Fig. 1c). Considering that the ethylene gas receptor in plants also contains Cu, copper nanoparticles can form a complex with ethylene gas (Aldewachi et al., 2018; Li & Suslick, 2018).

The electrical charge (ζ value) of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex solution was −0.27 ± 0.60 mV. Quantum dots possess hydroxyl and carboxylate groups on their surfaces, resulting in a negative zeta potential for CuNPs/N@CQDs. However, due to the amine groups of N@CQDs, the negative value of CuNPs/N@CQDs zeta potential remained low (Travlou et al., 2018; Zulfajri et al., 2019). After the exposure of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanoparticle solution to banana ripening, the zeta potential decreased significantly to −2.80 ± 0.78. Notably, ethylene gas acts as a weak electron donor according to Eq. 7, while noble metals and carbon quantum dots function as electron acceptors. Consequently, the zeta potential of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution will decrease after reacting with ethylene gas (Jourshabani et al., 2024; Samerjai et al., 2012).
C2H4+ O− C2H4O+ e− Eq. 7

To investigate the fluorescence behavior of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution, the emission spectrum of the solution was examined across the excitation wavelength range of 200–700 nm (Fig. 1d). The CuNPs/N@CQDs solution exhibited no fluorescence emission at any excitation wavelength other than 450 nm. Therefore, 450 nm was identified as the optimal excitation wavelength for the fluorescence emission of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution. Fig. 1e displays the fluorescence spectra of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex solution prior to and after being exposed to banana ripening. CuNPs/N@CQD has an excitation-dependent peak at 450 nm, which is attributed to carbon quantum dots, and an excitation-independent peak at λ = 500 nm (Aziz et al., 2019). After banana ripening, the excitation-independent peak of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanoparticle solution diminished, leading to a decrease in blue fluorescence and a color change from light green to dark green. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity ratio decreased from 1.825 to 1.194 after the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution interacted with ethylene gas, confirming the proper reaction with the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex. Also, the study of changes in the fluorescence spectrum of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution during the banana ripening process on storage days showed the gradual effect of ethylene gas on the reduction of the fluorescence spectrum. Specifically, with more ethylene gas production, the fluorescence spectrum showed a greater reduction (Fig. 1f). These results were consistent with the results of the color change of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator.

This fluorescence quenching is probably attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE), a mechanism where the absorption of the analyte overlaps with the emission spectrum of the fluorophore, leading to reduced fluorescence intensity. In this case, the formation of copper-ethylene complexes upon exposure to ethylene gas results in increased absorption in the visible region, which overlaps with the emission spectrum of the carbon quantum dots. This overlap leads to efficient energy transfer from the excited-state carbon quantum dots to the copper-ethylene complexes, resulting in reduced fluorescence emission (Santiago Cintrón et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2023; Zu et al., 2017). However, given the spectral overlap, the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) participation in the observed decrease cannot be ruled out. In the FRET mechanism, energy transfer occurs directly between the CuNPs/N@CQDs complex and the complex formed between copper nanoparticles and ethylene gas. In order to determine exactly which method occurs in this mechanism, it is necessary to investigate the fluorescence lifetime (Zu et al., 2017).

The localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption spectrum of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution showed a red shift toward higher wavelengths upon exposure to banana ripening. Specifically, a shift occurred in the maximum absorption wavelength from 369 nm to 446 nm (Fig. 1g). Additionally, the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution had a sensitivity of 2.56 × 104 nm/RIU to ethylene gas. Nanoparticle aggregation occurs due to the interaction of ethylene molecules with the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution, resulting in the LSPR peak shifting toward higher wavelengths (Jain et al., 2008; Li et al., 2020). Notably, after the nanoparticle solution reacted with ethylene gas, the LSPR peak fell within the visible range. Consequently, the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex can be utilized for colorimetric identification of ethylene gas (Rodrigues et al., 2021).
The results of the elemental analysis of the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution by ICP-MS confirmed the presence of carbon, copper, oxygen, and nitrogen elements in the sample.

The results of the EDS elemental mapping analysis in Fig. 2 (A1-A7) showed that the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator is mainly composed of carbon (42.49 %), oxygen (31.78 %), nitrogen (6.88 %), sulfur (15.98 %), and copper (2.86 %), which have a uniform distribution and contrast in the indicator. The reason for the presence of sulfur in the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator compared to the CuNPs/N@CQDs solution was related to the presence of sulfuric ester in the agar polymer (Antohe et al., 2019). Also, the EDS analysis showed that the doping of the N element on the CQDs surface and the establishment of the CQDs complex with Cu were well done, which improved the fluorescence and LSPR properties.

The AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator exhibited noticeable color changes in response to different ethylene concentrations. By increasing the concentration of ethephon from 0.05 to 4.5 M, the brightness parameter (L*) of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator decreased from 72.59 ± 0.70 to 48.87 ± 0.57, while its redness (a*) and yellowness (b*) parameters increased from 0.25 ± 0.12 and 8.17 ± 1.28 to 3.36 ± 0.49 and 44.54 ± 1.39, respectively (Table 1 ). Therefore, the indicators underwent a color change from light green to yellow and finally turned brown. Fig. 2B demonstrates a strong relationship between the ΔE of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator and the ethephon content (R2 = 0.97). Notably, according to Zhang et al. (2010), one mM ethephon releases 1.15 μL/L of ethylene gas. As a result, the LOD and LOQ for ethylene detection using the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator were 9.94 and 30.12 μM, respectively.

Fig. 2C displays the FTIR spectra of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator prior to and after being exposed to banana fruit ripening. The peak at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the -OH stretching vibration owing to the existence of H2O molecules during the hydrothermal process, which decreased after exposure to banana ripening (Liu et al., 2020). The absorption bands at 2921 and 2855 cm−1 represent the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibration of the CH band, respectively, which increased after exposure to banana ripening. The bands at wavenumbers of 1638 cm−1 and 1072 cm−1 correspond to the NH and CN stretching bonds, respectively. The increase in the intensity of these bands after exposure to banana ripening was related to the production of ethylene. The CuO absorption band can be observed at the wavenumber of 580 cm−1 (Das et al., 2020; Moradi et al., 2019). The most significant absorption bands are Cu-O-H and CH. The Cu-O-H bonding appeared at 931 cm−1, which intensified in the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator spectrum following the exposure to banana ripening due to the bonding of copper with carbon and hydrogen in the ethylene gas molecule. Also, the appearance of a new band in the spectrum of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator after being exposed to banana ripening (Fig. 2C, b) at 772 cm−1 is associated with the cis-C-H bond, which is probably related to the exposure of the indicator to ethylene released from banana (Awwad & Albiss, 2015; Nandiyanto et al., 2023). Furthermore, the stretching-vibrational band at 1380 cm−1 is related to the CuS bond. The presence of this band in the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator prior to being exposed to banana ripening is because of the sulfuric esters in the galactan of the agar polymer. The slight increase in the intensity of this band after banana ripening is due to hydrogen sulfide being produced as a biological molecule in the modulation of the fruit ripening process (Antohe et al., 2019; Corpas et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2012).

Fig. 3a 1 and 2 show the overall XPS spectrum of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator prior to and after being exposed to banana fruit ripening. The three main peaks in this spectrum include C1S, N1S, and O1S, which can be seen at 284.5, 400.1, and 532.2 eV binding energies, respectively. The XPS spectrum also exhibits copper and sulfur elements. The binding energy at 284.5 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of C1S is associated with the C-C/C=C bond (Fig. 3b-1 and b-2), which had its intensity decreased after banana ripening. The high-resolution spectrum at 400 eV indicates the NC bond (Fig. 3c-1 and c-2) (Lu et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2022). The high-resolution spectrum of oxygen (Fig. 3d-1) shows the C-OH/C-O-C bond at 532 eV. The intensity of this peak increased after bananas ripening due to the exposure of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator to ethylene gas (Fig. 3d-2) (Ma et al., 2023). Fig. 3e-1 displays the high-resolution spectrum of S2P, revealing 4 main peaks at binding energies of 160.5, 164.1, 167.1, and 170.5 eV. These peaks correspond to CuS (S2P3.2), CS (S2P3.4), SO (S2P3.2), and SO4 2− based on the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NISTXPS) database, respectively. The intensity increasing of these peaks after being exposed to banana ripening may be due to the carbon in ethylene gas as well as sulfur in hydrogen sulfide gas. During fruit ripening, as shown in the FTIR results, in addition to abundant production of ethylene, hydrogen sulfide gas is also produced in small quantities (Fig. 3e-2) (Fantauzzi et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). The main point of this spectrum relates to copper, as shown in Fig. 3f. The four main peaks in this spectrum are observed at binding energies of 931, 932, 947, and 952 eV. The binding energies at 931 and 933 eV are related to the Cu (OH)2 and Cu(2p3/2), respectively, indicating the establishment of a bond between copper and ethylene gas molecules, and the intensity of these peaks increased after banana ripening, which is consistent with FTIR results (Gard et al., 2020). Additionally, the binding energies of 947 and 952.1 eV correspond to Cu2p3/2 sat (according to the NISTXPS database), and Cu2p1/2 in copper ions, respectively (Peng et al., 2020).

During banana ripening, various volatile compounds, including esters, alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, acids, and other minor compounds, are synthesized, which increases with the increase of ethylene gas (Zhu et al., 2018). Table 2 displays the response of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator to possible volatile compounds produced during banana fruit ripening. As can be seen, there is no significant response of the indicator to hexane, acetic acid, ethanol, CO2, and methane. However, the indicator behaved differently when exposed to H2S gas and changed its color. This behavior aligns with our previous study, which demonstrated that this indicator responds to H2S gas and undergoes color changes (Lohrasbi Nejad & Shekarchizadeh, 2024).

The ability of an indicator to identify the desired compound in a complex environment is very important. Table 3 displays the color characteristics of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator after being exposed to various compounds and a 2.5 M ethephon solution. Notably, there is no significant difference in the color of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator when hexane, acetic acid, and ethanol are present. However, L*, a*, and b* parameters of the prepared indicator significantly alter when various concentrations of hydrogen gas are introduced. This behavior highlights the sensitivity of the indicator to hydrogen sulfide gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response time of the indicator to ethylene gas
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2D shows the changes in the ΔE parameter of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator when exposed to ethylene gas at different times. The results showed that the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator responded to the lowest detectable concentration of ethylene gas in about 120 min, indicating the ability of the indicator to respond appropriately to ethylene gas.

The relatively slow response time of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator can be attributed to the use of ethephon as the ethylene source in this study. Ethephon hydrolyzes slowly to release ethylene gas, which subsequently delays the interaction between ethylene and the indicator. This factor is important to consider when interpreting the current results.

The results of the study of the changes in the color characteristics of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator at different temperatures showed that different temperatures did not have a significant effect (p > 0.05) on the parameters L*, a*, and b* (Table 4 ). This suggests that the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicators maintain their stability across various temperatures, making them suitable for determining ripeness and spoilage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Banana ripening monitoring using AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator
ce:section.ce:para: The color parameters of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator used for bananas stored at 4 °C and 25 °C are shown in Fig. 4 . During the 10-day storage of bananas at ambient and refrigerator temperatures, the L* parameter decreased to 70.83 ± 1.40, and 70.95 ± 0.45, while the a* and b* parameters increased to 3.62 ± 0.40, 0.52 ± 0.04, and 29.83 ± 1.86, 22.48 ± 0.83 at ambient and refrigerator temperatures, respectively. In fact, due to the impact of the target analyte on the nanoparticles, accumulation occurred, leading to changes in the dielectric coefficient and resulting in a refractive index and color changes of the indicator (Fu et al., 2020; Mohammadi & Jafari, 2020). The intensity of ΔE for the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator after being exposed to bananas stored at ambient temperature was higher (30.44 ± 1.66 versus 24.84 ± 0.88 on 10th day), likely due to the faster ripening speed of bananas at this temperature.

Fig. 4 also shows the color changes of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator during the banana ripening process. The color of the indicator changed from light green on day zero to yellow and finally to brown by the 10th day. Specifically, the color changed to yellow starting on the 3rd day for bananas stored at ambient temperature and on the 4th day for those stored at refrigerator temperature. Low temperatures slow down enzymatic processes, including respiration rate and ethylene production (de Vasconcelos Facundo et al., 2012).

Comparison of the ΔE parameter changes of the indicator over time in damaged and intact packages of bananas stored at ambient and refrigerated temperatures showed that there was no significant difference in the ΔE parameter between damaged and intact packaging (Fig. 5 ), and there was a similar color change trend in the indicator containing CuNPs/N@CQDs in both packaging conditions (p > 0.05). This could be due to the indicator's insensitivity to atmospheric gases and its low LOD and LOQ in response to ethylene gas.

Table 5 shows the changes in banana skin color during a 10-day storage period at refrigerator and ambient temperatures. During storage, the a* parameter increased from 6.36 ± 0.79 to 24.60 ± 0.65 at ambient temperature and to 21.72 ± 1.42 at refrigerator temperature, which is due to the decomposition of chlorophyll and the increase of carotenoids. However, the b * and L * values decreased from 69.04 ± 1.82 and 46.70 ± 1.30 to 37.17 ± 2.34 and 24.10 ± 4.18 at ambient temperature and to 24.13 ± 0.93 and 9.16 ± 1.70 at refrigerator temperature, respectively, because of the formation of brown stain on the banana skin and the onset of the ripening cycle (Mazen & Nashat, 2019). The cause of brown spots is physiological disorders after harvesting, resulting from the activation of enzymes related to browning, such as polyphenol oxidase and peroxidase, which convert phenolic compounds to orthoquinone and cause browning (Nadafzadeh et al., 2018). The intensity of color change and browning is greater in banana fruits that are stored at refrigerator temperature because bananas are cold-sensitive products that suffer from cold damage at temperatures below 10 °C. The activation of polyphenol oxidase at low temperatures leads to the oxidation and polymerization of phenols, leading to the synthesis of dopamine, the main phenolic molecule in the banana skin (Hashim et al., 2012; Hashim et al., 2013).

The amount of reducing sugars in bananas significantly increased during storage from 418.23 ± 0.48 ppm on day zero to 1251.53 ± 2.52 ppm at room temperature and 1025.07 ± 0.69 ppm at refrigerator temperature (Fig. 6a). These total soluble sugars in the banana fruit not only contribute to its taste but also play an essential role in adapting to stress by affecting energy metabolism and osmotic regulation. Glucose, fructose, and sucrose are the three main sugars in bananas, and their amounts increase during the ripening process (Zhu et al., 2018). This increase is attributed to higher ethylene gas levels and the activity of enzymes responsible for starch hydrolysis. Additionally, the rate of sugar decomposition by respiration decreases during ripening (Alam et al., 2021; Kheng et al., 2012; Watharkar et al., 2020). Interestingly, bananas stored at refrigerator temperature exhibited lower amounts of reducing sugars. This reduction is due to the inhibitory effect of cold temperatures on starch-to-sugar conversion and the decreased production of ethylene gas at lower temperatures (Chen et al., 2021; de Vasconcelos Facundo et al., 2012).

Fig. 6b shows the firmness of banana texture during a 10-day storage period at ambient and refrigerator temperatures. The texture strength of banana fruit reached from 7.60 ± 0.36 N on day zero to 3.53 ± 0.21 and 5.19 ± 0.50 N on the last day at ambient and refrigerator temperatures, respectively. Several physicochemical changes may account for the reduction of firmness, such as the breakdown of starch into soluble sugars, the conversion of insoluble protopectin to soluble pectin, the decrease in adhesion of the middle layer, and the water moving from the skin to the fruit pulp. These changes ultimately result in a watery and wrinkled texture (Kheng et al., 2012; Kurnianto et al., 2020; Rahman et al., 2013). Youryon and Supapvanich (2017) noted that bananas are ripe when their tissue hardness is approximately 6 N, and they become overripe when the hardness reaches around 5.60 N. Therefore, bananas kept at ambient temperature until the 3rd day and those stored at refrigerator temperature until the 6th day were suitable for consumption. In these cases, the color of the indicators turned dark yellow. However, bananas stored at ambient temperature and in the refrigerator reached an overripe state on the 4th and 7th days, respectively, eventually losing their suitability for consumption. Consequently, the color of the indicators gradually shifted to dark brown.

A ripeness indicator based on copper and nitrogen carbon quantum dot nanoparticles was designed to control the amount of ethylene gas resulting from the ripening process of bananas. The findings of this work reveal a direct relationship between the intensity of color changes (ΔE) of the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator and the concentration of ethylene gas. The indicator's color change mechanism relies on the impact of ethylene gas on the surface of nanoparticles and their accumulation, as well as the refractive index and dielectric coefficient conversion. In addition, ethylene gas obtained from the banana ripening process can reduce the emission spectrum of the CuNPs/N@CQDs nanocomplex and diminish the intensity of blue fluorescence light. A strong correlation exists between the decrease in firmness of banana tissue, the increase in reducing sugars, and the color change of the indicators. This suggests that the AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs indicator can accurately determine the optimal time to consume banana fruit stored at both ambient and refrigerator temperatures. Specifically, the indicator's color turns dark yellow when the banana fruit is ripe, and it becomes brown when it is overripe. The AGH-CuNPs/N@CQDs ripeness indicator has several benefits, including selectivity, affordability, simple synthesis, and non-destructive detection of fruit ripening with the naked eye. However, future studies are necessary to address potential limitations and further optimize the indicator's performance, including its sensitivity under varying environmental conditions and its applicability to a broader range of fruits.

Sepideh Lohrasbi Nejad: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hajar Shekarchizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
